speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two of the Survivors of the Shoah. The interview is Itka Zygmuntowicz, April 19, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: English.

speaker: interviewee
text: English. Oh, I forget. See? I'll switch it for one. The most important, what the market had, that it provided relationships, which we and people, like the people, peasants, which otherwise wouldn't have too much occasion to meet Jewish people. And it provided business. You see, now when there's no Jewish people here, there's no more market here. It's, it's become a park now. So it's-- everything is gone now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itka, you are a fashion designer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say some words about clothes? Did you have your favorite clothes at the time before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I came of a unique family, because my grandmother was a daughter of a [INAUDIBLE]. And the mother and the father was extremely orthodox. Therefore, my grandmother would wear the traditional orthodox gown.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was wearing a wig, but not to beautify herself. Because the woman at that time, as I remember them, the religious, Jewish women, will wear either a wig or a kerchief, you know? And she would wear very conservative clothes. She was very modest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of all, she lived with the Jewish laws, customs, and tradition. We strictly observe the Jewish holidays, the Sabbath, and all those things. In fact, as I-- previous testimony, she would not permit me to take a piece of-- a bite of food without saying a, saying a prayer. And the stories which I told previously I'm not going to repeat. At the other hand, my parents was religious. By today's standards, they would-- could almost call them orthodox. Then they didn't. They didn't  wear the traditional garb. However, they observed the Jewish laws and traditions. My-- we get a, a Jewish education, you know, and this education in the Pole-- in the school, which I attended. And we observed the holidays. And speaking about clothes, I'll tell you a very little story. When we lived in Poland, regardless how rich or poor we were, the Jewish people, twice a year, and regardless how much clothes you had, the Jewish people, for Pass-- for Passover, for the high holidays-- Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kuppur-- the children would always get new clothes. And at that time, we didn't have big department stores or markets. So therefore, we used to go to the dressmaker to get made new dresses, to the shoemaker to get new shoes. And I remember. I used to get patent leather shoes with white socks. And it was-- and one time, I got a dress, a two-piece dress, with a sailor color, you know, from navy blue gabardine with white stripes on the collar and a pleated skirt. And when my mother went with me to the dressmaker to pick it up, I I was felt-- came home and tried it on. And I felt like the whole world belongs to me. I had such a beautiful new dress. And as a child, I wanted to go out and show all my friends. And my mother said, [? Itka, ?] I would advise you not to put on the dress now, because it's for the holidays. In case something happens, you will not have it for the holidays. But I insisted. So my mother said, I guess you're only going to learn from your experiences. And but it's your responsibility. And I promised. I said, mommy, I will not-- I will take good care. And I went out. And I was a tomboy. And we had in Ciechanow, a river. It's called [? Ladinya ?]. And at the banks of the river, there was, you know grass, a lot of trees. And the kids said, [? Itka, ?] let's look down. And we'll play there a little. And I went. And then they challenged. Let's see who could climb up fastest on the tree. And I was a tomboy and fast. So I started to climbing. I was beating somebody else. And I don't know whether they want to scare me or what was the reason. They said, Itka, your mother is coming. I got so frightened. And I remembered I wasn't supposed to do that. So I wanted to get off fast. And I ripped my skirt. Cannot imagine how I felt. Regretful, felt guilty for not listening to my mother. And I-- it was pleated. So I took-- put over the pleat like this with tears in my eyes. And I came home. My mother looked at me. She didn't have to ask anymore. She said, take off the dress. And I'll fix it for you. And regardless, whether you going to-- you can have a choice. Wear your old dress or the new when it's fixed. But you're going to go to synagogue for the holiday. And she said, remember Itka, there is a lesson here. You never take chances on something you cannot afford to lose. And I believe that this influenced me so that till this day, when I-- I would never go gamble in a casino. I don't want to leave my life in the hands of chance. I want to be in control of my life. Because 19-- from September 1, 1939, when the Germans, Germany invaded Poland, till I was liberated during Passover, April 26, 1945, by the Swedish Red Cross, I have seen all those values that my family taught me. And I remembered one thing. I don't want to leave to chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because regardless of what I do or who told me what to, the consequences are mine. And I cannot afford to lose two things-- my life, my self-respect, my dignity, and my freedom. On those things, I do not take a chance. So that story is always connected, and with clothes, you know? And-- Returning to your Jewish school, do you remember the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember. And I was a naughty girl. Because when they spoke in Yiddish and tell stories about Jewish history and everything, I was interested. When I had to make prayers, and I didn't understand yet what they meant, I never wanted to say something unless I understood it. So I felt a little re-- reluctant to do it. And but nevertheless, I've learned how to write Yiddish. Till this day, I know how to write Yiddish. And looking back, my family and my religion had the greatest impact on me, really. Because-- and the reason why it is, because I grew up in a home where there were diet-- the kosher dietary laws. Then there was a restriction, as you all know-- the ones who know about Jewish laws-- that after meat, you have to wait six hours til you can eat dairy, after dairy, a half an hour or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: So even if I wanted to do it, I couldn't do it. So I learned to say no to myself. Also, it influenced me because whenever there was a holiday, my-- I saw the rituals. and being born during Passover, heard the many stories. And every year I heard the prayers-- next year in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did not-- it never entered my mind that I will be one who will live to see after two thousand year, you know, to see the rebirth of our-- of Israel, you know, in 1948. I just-- it grieves me that my family did not survive to see this year and so many others. And it is because what they were not privileged to do that I came here for those who-- well, not privileged. When the Israelites were in, in, in Egypt, by Pharaoh, and when they were li-- when they were liberated, with-- prayed-- before they were liberated, prayed, my people go. Now we pray, let my people know what's happened to us. And I can tell you this, that the anti-Jewish laws during Pharaoh pale in comparisons with the law that's happened during Hitler. Because Pharaoh just singled out Jewish male children. And Hitler singled out all Jews. And not only did he single them out for dead and they legalized murder, that to, to murder-- to murder, mass murder of the Jews was legal. But for a Jew to survive, even a child, was illegal and punishable by death. But that was still not the worst. The worst was that they tried to dehumanize us and destroy our image of ourself and of God by telling us that we are subhuman, that we are vermin. And when we came to the concentration camps, and for those who were fortunate enough to be taken into the camp, they put on tattoos, like this here. And from then on, the did-- they always called [NON-ENGLISH], which means prisoner, 25673. That was my number. And whenever they did that, I said always under my breath, [? Itka-- ?] because that's what my family called me-- I said, I am not a numbers. Numbers cannot feel. They cannot think. They cannot act.

speaker: interviewer
text: But people can. People beget people. And people can be creators or destroyers of life. This is what I have seen. And the comparison is that a man like Raoul Wallenberg, right? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He saved so many Jewish people because he cared. On the other hand, the Nazi doctor, Josef Mengele, is responsible for the death of over 400,000 people, including thousands of people from the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] where I was before I came to Auschwitz-Birkenau and my entire family. So I have seen that men can be creators and destroyers of life. We have the potential for both. And the difference is how much do we care. Understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're here. We are at, at the front of the old Jewish bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here was a Jewish bakery. And the name of the bake-- the man who had the bakery was [? Bianco. ?] And here was-- what I have memories is that during Passover, because we kept the Jewish tradition and laws, we didn't-- we only ate matzo for Passover. We didn't' eat bread, according to the Jewish tradition.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was the-- when Passover was over, there used to be a long line of people. They-- and they used to buy bread. Because everybody, after not eating eight days bread, everybody was longing for eating bread. So I remember as a child, we used to come here and have bread. And one thing was funny, that sometimes on the road, people were so anxious that they would take off a little piece and eat it before they even brought it home. And now, this-- nobody lives here. It's empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there are very few Jewish houses and no Jewish people left here-- none. Everything was wiped off. However, further down, there is a building, a huge building comparing to-- in the-- in comparison to the others, which still is the original building from before the war. There was a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a big building. And I remember. When we come to it, I'll tell you the story about it. OK? It's a little further down. Where is-- shall we walk there, or? Yes, I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to tell the story now? Wooden homes, and one of was our home, I believe. But there's none. They are torn down because all the wooden buildings are torn down. So the [INAUDIBLE], where I lived the last few years when Germany came in, before Germany came in, I no longer see here. It was right next to the [INAUDIBLE]. So when we get there, I'll show you. OK? Thank you. [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] OK. This is the building where there was the [INAUDIBLE] and the drugstore. And there was a lot of Jewish people living in this building. And when the Nazis came, came in and they started to bombard because, you know, the war was going on, I remember my family came here to hide in the cellar. And we lived-- when you turn my hand from the back around this area. But all the wooden house, houses, that were standing are no longer existed. They were torn down. But I remember that all we had to do to go out from our house-- and it was near [INAUDIBLE] street. So it's between when you now, all you see there. This is where we lived. And here, we were hiding. And then when the, when the Nazis took Poland, Warsaw, in 19-- September 1, 19-- September 28, 1939, after that time, we could no longer move-- go from place to place. That was the last time where I could walk freely or go to, go to visit my family, my friends, my relative. We were [INAUDIBLE]. And I'm not going to elaborate, because I spoke about it before. Because this is when they started the actual destructions here. So my family, my aunt, my mother's sister, Siri--, Shmuel Pazdziorek, [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] Ester [INAUDIBLE] Shmuel Pazdziorek also lived in Ciechanow, Poland, not far from here. And they were the parents of seven children, six daughters and one son. Fortunately, in 19-- wait a second-- 1934, when I was eight years old, my cousin, who met a young man also native of Ciechanow, Poland-- and they fell in love. He, [? Chaim ?] [INAUDIBLE] emigrated with the first Maccabiah to Palestine. I think it must have been about 1932, and at that time was Palestine. Later, when he got a little established, she left. And I remember the whole family gathered. And everybody was crying and saying, God knows when we can see each other again. And my grandmother, who was a woman of deep faith, said children, please don't cry. With God, all things are possible. And because my cousin [? Ito, ?] named of the same great-grandmother-- the same name, the same birthplace-- left Ciechanow, Poland, she was the only one out of seven children that survived. And she could never come back to Ciechanow, Poland. There is no more Jewish, Jewish Ciechanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: How sh-- this river is called [? Ladinya ?] in Ciechanow, Poland. And when I was a child, we used to come here very often, many different capacities. People came here, some to bait. Here, no father, there was-- people came Saturday after they came form the synagogue. And they had the meal. They used to take a rest. And then towards in the evening, before they went to the synagogue again, they would come here. And it would be like a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Avenue.

speaker: interviewee
text: An avenue where young boys and girls would meet, where parents and children would walk. And I remember very often holding my father's, my mother's hand and walking here. And this is where I used to play, as I told you before. I used come to play here. And farther down on the other the bank, there were lots of trees and used to come here and play and run and climb trees. And this is one of those trees. I tore my, my sailor suit and learned the lesson never to take chances, as my mother said, on things you cannot afford to lose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here also, because most people in Ciechanow, Poland, didn't have the time when I was child, didn't have running water in the house. Many people came here to wash their clothes in the river, you know? Because there w-- otherwise, we used to take river from from a nearby well. And either we would carry ourself, or we had a man that would carry on, on his back. He had like a special thing to carry the water, the pails of water, and bring. And here, we played. And this, this is a hedge here-- wonderful memories. And tragically and ironically, here where [? I spent ?] here where I spent so many happy years and happy memories, when you just turn around, you'll see a castle where as a child-- you see this castle? As a child, we used to run there and play.

speaker: interviewee
text: This castle, [? a few-- ?] it was empty. And this castle, on October 15, 1941, in middle of the night, the Nazis broke in by force to our home and yelled on the gunpoint with dogs, rifles, and yelled [NON-ENGLISH]. And they chased us out. And we didn't have a chance to take too much. We left only very few belongings. And we had to-- they forced us in a truck. And they brought us here. And on October 15, 1941, as I said, was the last time I saw Ciechanow. And now after 55 years, I'm back. And my heart is breaking, because the city's here. But a city that was with so many Jewish people, that vibrated with life, children, with tradition, where my family lived for-- on my mother's side for ne-- from almost from the 16th century-- they came from Germany to Poland. I come here and nothing is left, only memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itka,-- do you want to add anything to what I asked? Well, maybe you can say, what is the most important thing in life for you? Or what is your message for the end of the interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust has taught me many lessons. The most important lesson it has-- that has-- it has told me that we must never be silent and indifferent when people suffer any place in the world. We have to be in solidarity with the oppressed and never with the oppressor, to stand up for justice, not to be afraid to speak up, and not to wait til it becomes so horrible where we have lost control of our life. Right in the beginning, we have to speak up and stand up for justice for everyone. And other lessons that I have learned, after I have lost everything, I have learned not to take anyone or anything for granted. Because people are the only thing that you cannot replace. You can replace-- you can build other homes. You can have other possessions. So what I have learned that there are things you cannot buy without money. Money's important to buy the necessities. But there are things you cannot buy for money. And no power on Earth can ever place the irreplaceable loss of the six million, including the people from Ciechanow, where previously lived so many people. Less than 200 survived. And most of those survive the USSR, ironically, only because part of them were sent to the-- were not during Nazi Germany here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all have an obligation. We cannot change the past. But we have all to be responsible for the present and for the future. And we have the power to protect the world and to be our brother's keeper. All by omission, we become our brother's killer. So silence is golden but not when people suffer. This what I have learned. And never again, never again can this happen. Because now I know that education alone is not enough. Religion alone is not enough .

speaker: interviewee
text: Menschlichkeit, which means humanness, because without menschlichkeit, all science, religion, and education fails. This I have seen in Auschwitz. Because Dr. Mengele was-- had two-- the Nazi doctor who made the selections who shall go to the gas chambers and who should live to suffer another day, he's responsible for so many deaths and for experiments on twins and other horrible things that he did. And yet he had two doctor, doctor degrees.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything can be used for good or evil. It all depends on us. We cannot blame God for human failure. Thank you. And I hope that no one will ever have to suffer like I. In conclusion, I want to say a little poem.

speaker: interviewee
text: My body belongs to me. To God belongs my soul. To my children belongs my inheritance. My love belongs to all. You cannot love Go and hate people. It's a contradiction. And when there is no love, there is destruction. Thank you. And God bless you all. And thank you for the wonderful work that you are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
